content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 9 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1CO_009_page_001.jpg

1. Am I not free? Am I not an apostle? Have I not seen Yeshua the Lord of us? Are you not the work of me in [the] Lord?

2. If to others I am not an apostle, yet certainly to you I am; for the seal of the apostleship of me you are in [the] Lord.

3. The defense of me to the [ones] examining me is this:

4. Do we not have a right to eat and to drink?

5. Do we not have a right to take along a sister [as] wife, as also the rest of the apostles and the brothers of the Lord and Kephas?

6. Or only I and Barnabas — do we not have a right not to work?

7. Who serves as a soldier at his own expense? Who plants a vineyard and does not eat of the fruit of it? Or who tends a flock and does not drink of the milk of the flock?

8. Do I say these [things] according to man? Or does not the law also say these [things]?

9. For in the law of Moshe it has been written, "You shall not muzzle an ox [while it is] threshing." Is it for the oxen God is concerned?

10. Or does he say [it] altogether for our sake? For our sake indeed it was written, because the [one] plowing ought to plow in hope, and the [one] threshing [ought to thresh] in hope of sharing.

11. If we sowed to you the spiritual [things], [is it] a great [thing] if we reap from you the fleshly [things]?

12. If others share this right over you, do not we rather? But we did not use this right, but we endure all [things], lest we give any hindrance to the good news of the Christos.

13. Do you not know that the [ones] performing the sacred [duties] eat from the temple, [and] the [ones] attending to the altar share with the altar?

14. Thus also the Lord directed the [ones] proclaiming the good news to live from the good news.

15. But I have used none of these [things]. And I did not write these [things] that it should be done thus to me; for it [is] good for me rather to die than — my boast no one will empty.

16. For if I proclaim the good news, there is not for me a boast; for necessity is laid upon me, for woe [is] to me if I do not proclaim the good news.

17. For if I do this willingly, I have a reward; but if unwillingly, a stewardship has been entrusted [to me].

18. What then is the reward of me? That proclaiming the good news, I may present the good news without cost, so as not to make full use of the right of me in the good news.

19. For being free from all, I enslaved myself to all, that I might gain the more.

20. And I became to the Ioudaioi as a Ioudaios, that I might gain Ioudaioi; to the [ones] under [the] law as under [the] law — not being myself under [the] law — that I might gain the [ones] under [the] law;

21. to the [ones] without law as without law — not being without law of God but within law of Christos — that I might gain the [ones] without law.

22. I became to the weak, weak, that I might gain the weak. I have become all [things] to all [people], that I might by all means save some.

23. And all [things] I do on account of the good news, that I may become a fellow sharer of it.

24. Do you not know that the [ones] running in a stadium all run, but one receives the prize? Thus run, that you may obtain.

25. And everyone competing exercises self-control in all [things]. Those then [do it] that they may receive a perishable crown, but we an imperishable [one].

26. Therefore I thus run, not as uncertainly; thus I box, not as striking air;

27. but I discipline the body of me and make [it] a slave, lest having proclaimed to others, I myself become disqualified.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 — Fourfold rhetorical question:** Paul establishes his credentials: free, apostle, eyewitness of the risen Lord, church-founder. Each question expects a "yes."

2. **v.5 — "brothers of the Lord":** ἀδελφοὶ τοῦ κυρίου — Paul mentions Jesus's brothers as traveling missionaries, along with their wives. This confirms that Jesus's biological brothers were active in the early church leadership.

3. **v.9 — Deuteronomy 25:4:** Paul's exegesis: the law about not muzzling oxen applies "altogether" (πάντως) to human workers. This allegorical reading was common in Jewish exegesis.

4. **v.14 — "the Lord directed":** διέταξεν — Paul refers to a command of Jesus (cf. Luke 10:7, "the worker deserves his wages"). Paul knows this teaching of Jesus but deliberately chooses not to exercise the right it grants.

5. **v.20 — "as a Jew":** ὡς Ἰουδαῖος — the "as" (ὡς) is debated. Was Paul acting as if he were Jewish (implying he no longer was), or was he accommodating Jewish practices for missionary purposes? The latter is more likely — Paul remains Jewish but is flexible in practice.

6. **v.27 — "disqualified" (ἀδόκιμος):** The athletic metaphor concludes with a stark warning. The herald (κηρύξας) who announces the rules could himself be disqualified. Paul takes seriously the possibility of apostolic failure.

— EMET, AI Translator
